title: Quality Control & Consensus
concept: Quality control in annotation ensures high agreement and correctness among different human labelers.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main characteristic of the YOLO annotation format?

options.text: One text file per image containing normalized decimal coordinates
A single giant JSON file storing pixel coordinates
Structured XML files storing raw pixel bounding boxes

exercises.explain: YOLO format writes one compact TXT file per image with class IDs and normalized values.

cards.title: Who checks the checkers?
cards.body: If you ask five people to draw a box around a 'large tree', you will get five different boxes. Some will include the branches, some just the trunk, and some might miss the tree entirely. To train a reliable model, we need rules to ensure all annotators agree.

cards.title: Consensus Labeling
cards.body: Consensus Labeling is a quality control strategy where the same image is annotated by multiple independent labelers. The labels are then compared using metrics like Inter-Annotator Agreement to ensure consistency.

cards.title: A Jury Verdict
cards.body: Instead of relying on a single witness, a court uses a jury. If 10 jurors all look at the evidence and agree on the outcome, we have high confidence. If they split 50/50, we know the case is ambiguous.

cards.title: Gold Standard Data
cards.body: Project managers insert 'gold standard' images (already verified as 100% correct) randomly into an annotator's queue. If a worker fails to label the gold images correctly, they are flagged for retraining.

cards.title: Annotation is not simple
cards.body: You might think data labeling is a mindless task that requires no instructions. In reality, without a detailed Labeling Guide (e.g., 'Do we include the side mirrors of the car?'), your dataset will be noisy and confuse the model.

left.text:
Labelers draw conflicting boxes.
Ambiguous guidelines.
Result: Model learns messy boundaries.

right.text:
Labelers agree on boundaries.
Clear guidelines & gold standards.
Result: Clean, precise model signals.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary goal of consensus labeling?
exercises.explain: Consensus labeling cross-checks annotations across multiple people to identify noise, bias, and mistakes.

options.text: To identify and reduce human labeling errors by comparing reviews from multiple people
To double the training speed of the convolutional network
To combine multiple images into a single training file
To run model inference on multiple edge devices in parallel

exercises.prompt: True or False: A 'gold standard' image is a benchmark image used to test an annotator's performance.
exercises.explain: True. Gold standard images are pre-labeled, high-quality reference points used to grade and monitor labeling quality.
A labeling guide defines strict rules for edge cases, ensuring all annotators label consistently.
exercises.sentence: To ensure annotators draw boxes consistently, teams must write a detailed labeling ___.

exercises.prompt: Which metric measures how consistently two annotators label the same set of images?
exercises.explain: Inter-Annotator Agreement calculates the consensus rate between different human labelers.

exercises.prompt: You notice that your labeling team is splitting 50/50 on whether to label 'pickup trucks' as 'cars' or 'trucks'. What is the best corrective action?

options.text: Update the labeling guide with a clear rule (e.g., 'label pickup trucks as trucks') and retrain the annotators on this rule.
Delete all images containing pickup trucks from the dataset.
Let the model decide by randomly swapping labels during training.
Fire half the team to force consensus.

exercises.explain: Ambiguity in labeling is resolved by clarifying guidelines, which directly eliminates systematic noise in your dataset.
